video_title: Tyler Perry’s STRAW | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
Tarji DID do a great job. The first few minutes you get swept up in her characters struggles. The actress who plays bank Mgr. Turns in a great performance too.
The problem that occurs about 3/4 of the way through is (unfortunately) predictable of a lot of Tyler perry's materials and detracts from these performances. Perhaps in his attempts to rebel AGAINST racial stereotypes he creates material that can be transformative and allows so many actors the opportunity stretch and grow so we can really see what they're capable of, but then he resorts to/ relies upon these same stereotypes that reinforce instead of heal these rifts. I feel that this movie xould have had such a.more signicant impression w/o the (unnecessary) twist at the end.
IDK maybe it's timing, but right now we all have bigger enemies we need to unify against rather than allow them to divide us w/o doing their jobs for them. 
Nice piece of acting!

comment_text: It was good I don’t care. Tyler was a genius. He is not making the black woman bad. Because I can relate to all the women… Tarij did it! I got the movie. We are all in a way like Janiyah trying to struggle, survive, and take care of our love ones. But Janiyah didn’t process the pain and her mind did a 360. That’s what happens to black women, we suck things up and go. It wasn’t about the plight of the black woman but how we need to be a little bit kind because you don’t know how someone’s day is. Pure Genius

comment_text: God this movie hit home. My son was really sick the past year and part of 2025. The sleepless nights caring for your child, hospital stays, stressing about the medical bills. Applying for financial aid and waiting on the outcome. Luckily he is ok right now but it is expensive to be poor. I have an ok job and am struggling to get but by the grace of God and the skills he gave me but man it is so hard to work 2 jobs. Especially right now in the summer. Just trying to keep pushing on and working to pay the medical bills so I won't be sent to collections. Please pray for my son who is 6 so God can continue to watch over him and keep him healthy. If you are a struggling mom, or dad, or grandparent or foster parent, I see you, I hear you and I feel you. Sending lots of love and good spirits your way, hopefully one day we can be on the other side of things.

comment_text:
I appreciated and enjoyed this Tyler Perry film. HOWEVER... (Spoilers), I can't help but clearly see it is the almost the same as the movie BREAKING with actor, John Boyega from 2022. The movie Breaking fell a little short and not everyone saw it, but I surely did. Tyler Perry copied so much from that movie it's embarrassing. Both characters are single parents to a young daughter, both were mistaken for robbing a bank, both had a standoff with police and FBI, both wanted a small amount of money (he wanted the $892 the VA owed him, she wanted to cash her $521 paycheck), both had fake, mistaken bombs, both commanded the bank blinds be closed, both bonded with the bank branch manager, both bonded with a police detective, both ended up breaking and reaching their last straw after being mistreated by organizations and people. Heck, even the titles are similar... "Breaking"... "Straw." 
To be even more Frank about it even the movie Breaking was inspired by John Q. Nonetheless, although it was not an original idea, I still found it entertaining and engaging.

video_title: Tyler Perry’s STRAW | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
I hated Teyana Taylor’s part, it was giving shallow and cliché. But Taraji… Taraji didn’t act. She bled.

I found myself weeping like a child, bowling my eyes out. The tension, her wounds wrapped in her breath. There’s something about the way she looks at you, not with her eyes, but with her scars and pain. You see her, not as a character, but as a mirror: every betrayal, every compromise, every desperate silence you’ve buried deep.

In her pain she sought freedom but she lost it completely, together with her bundle of happiness and pain. 

Indeed when it rains it pours,because life doesn’t wait for you to heal before it sends another blow. Misfortune has a haunting sense of timing—it finds you barefoot, tired, and one breath away from breaking.

Two jobs. Ten years. And still nothing.
What kind of world demands so much of a person and gives her so little?

And then I looked at myself.
I remembered my mother.

While Straw is fiction, Taraji’s truth is not. Her truth was a sermon. Her brokenness preached louder than any pulpit. And we must listen.

Her end must not just break our hearts. It must break our indifference,  this film remind us:Kindness is not charity. It is repayment—for the private wars many are fighting.

For all the single mothers out there struggling, this hits so close to home. This is really what it's like out here for a lot of us. If something happened to my baby, I'd have nothing more to lose. Nothing.

video_title: Tyler Perry’s STRAW | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
As a Black viewer who genuinely wants to see our stories told with depth and realism, I find it hard to connect with Tyler Perry’s recent works—like “Straw.” The plots often feel forced, exaggerated, or completely detached from how real people behave. For example, a character going through intense desperation somehow turning into a killer and a thief, while dealing with so much? It just doesn’t add up, and it weakens the message.

This isn’t about race—it's about storytelling. I’ve called out white directors for similar issues, and I’ll do the same here. Black stories deserve better. We need layered narratives, not just trauma mixed with sudden unrealistic plot twists. Tyler Perry has a massive platform and the resources to give us excellence, but too often, it feels like we’re being handed rushed inspiration over solid storytelling. It’s okay to expect more.

video_title: Tyler Perry’s STRAW | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text: Omg..I almost stopped watching in 10 minutes in because I was already crying...this punches you right in the heart. Some will not feel this movie, but I've been down. I've had days where EVERYTHING went wrong. I've had days when I didn't have money for gas or medicine. Both my children had free lunch at school because I couldn't pay for it. But I stayed with the movie through the tears and if Teraji doesn't get an Oscar then it's rigged. Her performance was deep, real and spot on. I'm so sorry for her, for how she was treated, for her shitty boss, for her shitty landlord....all of it. She might not be real, but im real and I've felt her pain.....Wow. Tyler Perry you have insight like no body else and it's not about color, im a 61 year old white woman and have been treated the same way. Profoundly moving.

comment_text:
I feel this needs to be said.

After watching this movie, most people will sympathise with her but will see themselves as her.
The point of the movie isn't that you get sympathy if you "snap", 
we are supposed to represent the people in the crowd, every little way you treat someone builds up throughout their day and lives, you never know what could be happening in someone's life so treat people with kindness, whether it's letting someone go before you in a que or on the road or deciding to be nice and forgiving about something small rather than lashing out and insulting people. 
Simply giving up your seat for someone who looks tired or deciding to let something go just once, this selfish "I always come first" mentality is the reason people end up without help, because no one's concerned enough to help. 
And sometimes it's fine to try once to be kind to someone who was initially rude or mean to you, you'd be surprised how some "rude people" are kind and genuine when they're given a chance.
It's better to have a friend than to make a list of enemies.

I'm not saying to be a pushover, you can be kind but also guarded.

video_title: Tyler Perry’s STRAW | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
This is a DAMN good movie, and hits HARD for anyone who has almost faced homelessness. That fear of knowing you wont have a safe place to lay your head in a few hours.

The twist at the end was heartbreaking, and I can perfectly see how anyone, much less a single black mother from poverty can spiral like this, at no choice of her own.

This was one movie I can truly say was a work of art...and I've only said that about 3 other movies in my lifetime. It's number one on NetFlix right now for a reason.

It hits all the chords, and tugs seriously at your heartstrings-showing this could happen to ANY single parent on the edge of poverty. Normally I dont like Tyler Perry movies, but he hit it out of the PARK with this work of art. WELL DONE good sir. You poured your heart into this, and it feels like it was based on a true story. Well done.

Keep your chins up, everyone. This could happen to you tomorrow.

comment_text:
I've just watched it.
Movie, "Straw" by Tyler Perry taught me that as a woman or a mom or a parent is, "have money & time management". Movie is similar to "John Q" Movie acted by Denzel Washington. Somehow I found it exaggerating at first.. questioning "the gun side where she held in the bank", "all along she's been imagining she was with her child not knowing her child was dead til the end" and "child doesn't have money for lunch and hasn't paid her rent due" lol. Anyway well done Tyler 👏. I hope this gives a positive light to women or mothers out there including men.

video_title: Tyler Perry’s STRAW | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
Straw does something bold and necessary by holding up a mirror to the raw, layered, and often overlooked experiences of Black women. This film explores the day-to-day struggles we face mentally, physically, and emotionally. It sheds light not just on the burdens we carry in silence, but also on the internal dynamics we navigate among ourselves as Black women. It’s honest, uncomfortable in the right ways, and deeply relatable.

There’s a powerful twist that caught me off guard, and it left me thinking long after the credits rolled. I was surprised to see the low ratings on Rotten Tomatoes, especially considering it’s currently ranked number one on Netflix. It makes you wonder if those critic scores truly reflect the voices of people who understand or even try to understand the film’s perspective. Sometimes, it feels like Rotten Tomatoes is just a place where people go to tear down movies that weren’t made for them.

Regardless of who wrote, directed, or produced it, credit is due. The cast did an incredible job portraying the complexity, vulnerability, and strength of Black women. It’s a film that speaks to us, and I can see how those outside our community might not fully grasp the layers. And that’s okay. But that doesn’t make it any less valid or powerful.

If you're a Black woman like me, this movie will hit home in more ways than one. And even if you’re not, it’s still worth watching with an open heart.  Excellent job, Tyler Perry and cast!
